canonical_name: U.S. Electricity Market and Grid Infrastructure Trends
facts: U.S. electricity consumption reached record levels in 2022 at approximately 4,067 billion kWh.
National electricity demand is projected to climb to 4,165 billion kWh in 2025, reflecting roughly a 13% increase from 2022 to 2025.
The electrification of transportation via more electric vehicles (EVs) on the road and a shift to electric heat pumps is accelerating grid load.
The U.S. Energy Information Administration (EIA) notes that homes and businesses are using more electricity for transportation and heating, contributing to demand growth.
Data centers could consume around 9% of total U.S. electricity by 2030, driven by hyperscalers and AI workloads.
The Biden Administration announced $1.3 billion in late 2023 to expand transmission lines nationwide, adding an estimated 3.5 GW of transfer capacity.
In 2024, the U.S. Department of Energy committed $1.5 billion for new transmission projects under its Transmission Facilitation Program.
Goldman Sachs projects that 85–90 gigawatts of new nuclear generation capacity might be required globally to meet data center-driven demand.
Two new reactors at Plant Vogtle in Georgia came online in 2023 and 2024, marking the first large-scale U.S. nuclear units in 30 years.
The Department of Energy is actively supporting advanced reactor technologies, including small modular reactors, and securing high-assay low-enriched uranium fuel for next-generation plants.
Retail electricity prices in the U.S. have increased faster than general inflation since 2022, with a projected ~13% jump in the national average from 2022 to 2025.
EV charging networks and electrified heating are contributing to new heights in grid peak demand periods.
Utilities are deploying thicker-gauge wires, higher-capacity transformers, and advanced grid control systems in high-growth corridors to handle increased loads.
Public utility commissions are permitting rate increases specifically to fund critical transmission investments needed for grid modernization.
Some utilities are exploring vehicle-to-grid (V2G) integration, where EVs or customer-sited batteries could supply power back to the grid as distributed support resources.

Tech companies including Google, Microsoft, and Meta have announced 100% renewable energy goals and are buying vast quantities of wind and solar power through long-term power purchase agreements (PPAs) spanning 10–20 years.
Utilities are adding utility-scale batteries to store excess solar or wind energy and release it during peak demand periods.
For critical new loads like hyperscale data centers, utilities often build N+1 redundancy into serving substations and feeders to ensure uninterrupted power supply.
source_title: Surging U.S. Electricity Demand Amid an AI Data Center Boom
